findings.description: Four distinct tint/transparency specifications with different scopes (campaign headline D/M, Light Blue callout box, Golden Sand callout box, Wave top layers) are collapsed into a single generic rule. Each has a different target element and a different specific value (40%, 70%, 30%, 70% transparency) and should be represented as separately scoped rules or facets, not one undifferentiated rule.

new_rules.value: ≈40% tint of headline color (Gold or Brand Blue)

findings.description: u0005 ('No other ad-hoc tints — use palette values or these specified tints only') is a closure/prohibition statement distinct in kind from the preceding value-specific tint rules. It sets a boundary condition (must_not use unlisted tints) rather than defining an approved value, and should be modeled as its own rule rather than folded into rule_lisraya_approved_tint_levels.

new_rule.scope: all tint/transparency applications not explicitly listed

findings.description: The single rule carries hardness=hard_constraint and polarity=must for the whole cluster, but the source contains a mix of declarative value specs (headline/callout/wave tints) and an explicit prohibition (no ad-hoc tints). Governance facets (scope target, exact value, applicable element) are not captured per-unit, making it impossible to verify compliance against any individual specification.

Wave top layers specify '70% transparency', a distinct attribute from 'tint' used in the other three units. Clustering it under a rule named/classed for 'tint_levels' misrepresents scope; transparency should either be a separate class or explicitly noted as a related-but-distinct attribute.
